11 Case Study 1 DM Innovation Engine - YouTube.mp4
What you're looking at here is a finished value engine. I use the term 'finished' very loosely because no value engine is ever truly finished. But what you see here is something that has moved beyond kind of the post-it notes and the whiteboard. into a finalized document. All right. And so you can see here that we've leveraged a little bit of color coding to denote different teams. So in this case, where you see 'That' would be a leadership team where you see blue. 'That' is for our product team where you see green. 'That' is for our marketing team. You'll also see some of those familiar shapes, right? The pill shape for the. Starting point and the ending points, the start and the terminus. You'll also see these parallelograms where it's an input or an output document.
You'll also see just your normal kind of rectangle, square. If it's a stage or a process, you'll see the diamonds for the decision diamonds, those gateways. So all the different elements are there just to get cleaned up and made to look a little bit nicer. So I'll kind of walk through this one again, just so that you have another example. In this case, This particular innovation engine shows how we go about creating new workshops at Digital Marketers. We found that at Digital Marketer, workshops really are one of those areas where we truly create value. Not only do they create sellable product, but they're also kind of that starting point. For other derivative work that can become blog posts, social media content, tools, resources, all these things.
This is a really critical value creation process, so it made sense that we would look to document it. Starting at the start, we went through the same process here. We said, where does this process begin? Now, what was interesting is early on, everybody kind of thought that, well, it starts with identifying the subject matter experts. And we realized that we forgot about this idea of, wait— we need to figure out what's the topic going to be? What workshops are we going to create? And then we said, 'OK, well, who's responsible for this?' So the reason that you see this in purple is because we realize that this is so important that this should actually be determined at a leadership level. This should actually be determined with, for example, the head of product and programs.
It should be determined with the head of marketing, head of sales. Like this is something we need to talk about at our leader's meeting. So you can see here this workshop topics calendar. This is actually a document. So this is an input that gets created once per quarter at our quarter. Planning meeting usually thinking out— you know— for the next two to four quarters, right? What are we going to produce? So this is the starting point. The starting point of this particular value engine— digital marketers' innovation engine— is the creation of this input. This workshop topic calendar now, once this has been created, gets handed off to to the product team. That's why this is in blue, and their job is to identify a subject matter expert.
That's what SME, SME, is short for— that is a subject matter expert. In other words, who is going to actually produce this particular workshop? Now in the past, the subject matter expert would be selected by the product team and they would just go. What we realized again is some subject matter experts are really great to work with. They have really great content, others less so. And the person on the product team doesn't always know that. So it's such a critical component. If we don't have the right subject matter expert, we're not going to have a great workshop. We're not going to have a great product. So Leadership needs to engage here again to approve. And since this is a gateway, right, they could approve or not approve.
That's where you see this is shown in that diamond. So, if no, they do not approve, then it's back to the drawing board for the product team. To identify a new subject matter expert, if they do approve, then that is when a member of the product team knows that I can execute an outreach campaign. I can reach out to the subject matter expert. Now we have a process in place for this, not bothering to document all that here. That comes later. Once the outreach has taken place, then either the deal happens or it doesn't. Either the subject matter expert says, 'Yep, I'm in.' I can do it on the dates, or they can't. If they can't, well, back to the drawing board, right? With this line. If they can, then what happens next?
So again, at every stage is like, and then what? And then what? So the deal closed and then what? Then the product team needs to draft this positioning document. Now this positioning document we realized was a really critical point because it's this positioning document where the product team specifies what are the learning objectives, right? What is a student who attends this workshop? What are they going to know? That they didn't know before? What is the transformation that's going to take place? They're going to specify who is it for and who is it not for. They're going to specify, you know, what are the big hooks from a marketing perspective that marketing can really, really run with. Now, again, in the past, Marketing wasn't engaging until, believe it or not, the workshop was over.
We realized this was a problem, so we said, and then what? When these happen next, well, marketing needs to accept this positioning document. Marketing needs to engage and say, 'Yes, this is something we can sell.' Or, 'No, this is not a great hook. We need to go back to the drawing board. Try again. We're not going to shift the workshop topic. You know, the title, we're not going to try to get another subject matter expert. But. In terms of the learning objectives and those things, you know, we may need to clarify some things. This doesn't happen often, but it is a really good gateway before it moves forward. If it is accepted, then that's when this positioning document is actually created. You can see here it's reflected as this parallelogram, because it is an output.
Alright, it is a document, it is an output. Now, this document goes kind of into two different places. The product team will then draft and outline the learning objectives. And from that, they will work with the subject matter expert to produce the workshop outline, so the actual slide deck that will form the the creation of the workshop at the same time the marketing team takes this product positioning document and they draft the big idea worksheet, headline, and bullet points— basically the landing page copy, you know, initial announcement email. And then that worksheet is created. So both of these outputs are created from this initial product positioning document. At that point, everybody comes together and they all meet with the leadership team to basically say, 'Here's the outline.
Here's the big idea worksheet.' Here's the product positioning document. Does the outline deliver on the big idea? We realized that we needed this check here because we didn't want the marketing team promising something that the product team was not going to deliver. So, if ultimately, it's decided that no, these don't line up. We're making promises that we're not going to be able to keep. Well, that's when product and marketing need to get into a collaboration meeting. That's where you see this meeting is kind of, you know, part blue, part green. They need to go back to the drawing board until yes. I mean, we were beyond the point of no return. They got to make it work. Once everybody agrees that yes, it delivers, then what the marketing team will initiate is the promotion engine.
So this particular— this is a growth engine that actually promotes the workshop. So we have a process in place for that. And the product team will initiate the fulfillment engine. So the actual delivery and productization of this particular workshop. So. There's nothing really new here that you haven't seen before. Same shapes, same basic process and concept, same idea of an initial starting point. Also note that the end, the terminus, or, in this case, the termini, there are two possible. There are two ends to this particular value engine. Each of them will initiate an additional value engine. In the case of this terminus right here, we're initiating a fulfillment engine. And in the case of this terminus here, we're initiating another growth engine, which is the actual promotion of this particular program. So, same basic concepts, same idea, prettier colors, straighter lines, cleaner boxes, but you get the idea. The point is we're documenting these critical value creation processes within the business. And again, they all need a starting point and they all need to end. And generally, where one value engine ends, another one begins. If you can keep that in mind, then you are going to have all of your critical value creation processes documented.
